structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHRs) from a single health system spanning 36 months (Jan 2021–Dec 2023). All adult patients (≥18 years) discharged from hospital with complete symptom progression logs, demographic data, and 30-day post-discharge follow-up status. Exclude: patients with <2 symptom log entries during admission, transfers to another acute facility, or incomplete outcome data.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission to any hospital (including the originating system) within 30 calendar days of discharge. Verified against claims data and EHR readmission records. A patient is labeled positive if they were admitted with any diagnosis code within 30 days; negative otherwise.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split by discharge date to prevent information leakage from future admissions. Training set: patients discharged Jan 2021–Aug 2022 (20 months, ~60% of eligible discharges). Validation set: patients discharged Sep 2022–Dec 2022 (4 months, ~15% of eligible discharges). Test set: patients discharged Jan 2023–Dec 2023 (12 months, ~25% of eligible discharges). Temporal split is essential because (1) symptom progression is inherently sequential; (2) model selection must occur on data strictly after training to avoid using future information for hyperparameter tuning; (3) the deployment context involves predicting readmission risk at discharge, making temporal alignment realistic. Within each split, stratify by readmission status (positive/negative) to preserve class distribution across splits.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler for clinical labs, OneHotEncoder for categorical demographics, symptom log normalization) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Missing values in clinical features are imputed using median (numeric) or mode (categorical) computed from the training set. Symptom log sequences are padded or truncated to a fixed length (e.g., 100 time steps) using statistics derived from training data.
structured_choices.features: Logistic regression baseline features: (1) Demographics: age, sex, race/ethnicity (one-hot encoded); (2) Clinical summary: count of chronic conditions (Charlson index components), primary discharge diagnosis category, length of stay, discharge disposition; (3) Pre-discharge Labs: creatinine, hemoglobin, glucose (most recent value in 7 days prior to discharge). RNN features: (1) same demographic and clinical summary features as above, provided as static context; (2) Symptom progression sequence: 100 timesteps of symptom log entries, each timestep containing: symptom category (one-hot: fever, dyspnea, chest pain, etc.), severity score (0–10), treatment type (medication class, procedure), and time delta from previous entry. No feature contains the discharge date, readmission outcome, or any information recorded after discharge.
structured_choices.model: Bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM) with 2 hidden layers (128 units each), dropout (0.3), and attention mechanism over the symptom sequence. The architecture concatenates the attended symptom representation with static demographic/clinical features, passes through a dense layer (64 units, ReLU), and outputs a sigmoid probability. Training: Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-3), binary cross-entropy loss with class weight balancing, 50 epochs, early stopping on validation loss (patience 5 epochs).
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on identical demographic and clinical summary features (excluding the symptom sequence). Class weights are applied to account for imbalance (weight = (n_negative / n_positive) for the positive class). Hyperparameter tuning (L2 regularization parameter) is performed via grid search on the same validation set used for RNN selection, ensuring equivalent tuning effort. The baseline represents the standard-of-care approach and isolates the contribution of sequential symptom modeling.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), chosen because: (1) Readmission is the minority class; AUPRC is more informative than AUROC for imbalanced datasets; (2) Operational context: the health system's goal is to identify high-risk patients for intervention (precision matters to allocate limited resources) while capturing as many at-risk patients as possible (recall matters to reduce readmissions); (3) AUPRC is not threshold-agnostic but reflects the trade-off relevant to deployment (threshold can be tuned post-hoc based on intervention capacity).
secondary_metrics: AUROC — to confirm discriminative ability across the probability spectrum
F1-score at threshold optimized for validation set — to balance precision and recall numerically
Sensitivity (recall) at 90% specificity — to assess detection rate when false positive rate is controlled
Calibration (Brier score, calibration plot) — to ensure predicted probabilities align with observed readmission rates, critical for clinical risk stratification
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (Sep 2022–Dec 2022 discharges). The RNN hyperparameters (hidden units, dropout, learning rate schedule) and logistic regression regularization are tuned via grid search on validation set using AUPRC as the selection criterion. No part of the test set is examined during tuning.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (Jan 2023–Dec 2023 discharges) is held untouched until final model evaluation. After model selection and hyperparameter finalization, each model (RNN and logistic regression) is re-trained on the combined training + validation sets (to maximize training signal) and evaluated once on the test set. Results are reported on test set only. No threshold tuning or metric inspection occurs on test data.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Socioeconomic status (SES) and follow-up care access: (1) Stratify test set reporting by insurance type (proxy for SES) and discharge disposition (proxy for discharge support/follow-up access) to detect differential model performance; (2) Include postal code-level poverty index as a feature in both models to adjust for SES; (3) Document the availability and type of post-discharge follow-up care recorded in EHR (e.g., scheduled PCP visit, home health referral) and report model performance separately for patients with and without recorded follow-up plans; (4) Acknowledge that unmeasured SES factors and informal support networks are not controlled and discuss their potential impact on generalizability. The experiment will show whether the RNN outperforms logistic regression *within* the captured population; it does not establish causal mechanisms or generalizability to health systems with different SES distributions or follow-up infrastructure.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling

The experiment uses EHR data from a single health system spanning January 2021 through December 2023, covering approximately 100,000 discharge events. We include all adult patients (age ≥18) with complete symptom progression logs, demographic records, and verified 30-day post-discharge outcome status. Patients are excluded if they have fewer than two symptom log entries during their admission, were transferred to another acute facility (complicating the readmission endpoint), or have missing outcome data.

The readmission label is defined as binary: a patient is positive if they were admitted to any hospital within 30 calendar days of discharge (verified via EHR and claims data), and negative otherwise. This definition is operationally meaningful—it aligns with CMS and quality reporting standards—and provides a clear outcome for model training.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

To prevent information leakage and respect the temporal structure of the problem, we use a **temporal split by discharge date**:

- **Training set:** Patients discharged Jan 2021–Aug 2022 (~60% of discharges, ~20 months)
- **Validation set:** Patients discharged Sep 2022–Dec 2022 (~15% of discharges, ~4 months)
- **Test set:** Patients discharged Jan 2023–Dec 2023 (~25% of discharges, ~12 months)

This split prevents models from inadvertently learning patterns from future data and ensures that hyperparameter tuning occurs on data that comes after training—a requirement for unbiased model selection in temporal forecasting. Within each split, we stratify by readmission status to preserve class imbalance proportions, ensuring consistent evaluation metrics across sets.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Logistic Regression Baseline Features:**
- Demographics: age, sex, race/ethnicity (one-hot encoded)
- Clinical summary: Charlson comorbidity index, primary discharge diagnosis (categorized), length of stay, discharge disposition
- Pre-discharge labs: most recent creatinine, hemoglobin, and glucose (within 7 days prior to discharge)
- Socioeconomic adjustment: postal code-level poverty index

**RNN Features:**
- Same static demographic and clinical features as above (concatenated with learned representations)
- Symptom progression sequence: 100 timesteps of symptom log entries, each containing (1) symptom category (one-hot encoded: fever, dyspnea, chest pain, etc.), (2) severity score (0–10), (3) treatment type (medication class or procedure), and (4) time delta from the previous entry

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are fit on the training set only and applied consistently to validation and test sets. Sequences are padded or truncated to 100 timesteps using statistics computed from training data. Critically, no feature contains the discharge date, readmission outcome, or any information recorded after discharge.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Primary Model: Bi-LSTM with Attention**
- Architecture: 2 bidirectional LSTM layers (128 units each), dropout (0.3 per layer), attention mechanism over the symptom sequence
- Integration: Attended symptom representation concatenated with static features, passed through a dense layer (64 units, ReLU), sigmoid output
- Training: Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-3), binary cross-entropy with class weights, 50 epochs, early stopping (patience 5) on validation loss

**Baseline: Logistic Regression**
- Features: Identical demographic and clinical summary features (excluding the symptom sequence)
- Class balancing: Inverse frequency weighting applied during training
- Tuning: L2 regularization parameter tuned via grid search on the validation set, using AUPRC as the selection criterion

The logistic regression baseline is appropriate because it represents current clinical practice (demographics and aggregate clinical data) and isolates the value of sequential symptom modeling. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort on the same validation set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC)**

AUPRC is chosen for three reasons: (1) Readmission is the minority class, and AUPRC is more informative than AUROC for imbalanced data; (2) The operational goal is to identify high-risk patients for targeted intervention (precision matters to allocate resources) while capturing as many at-risk patients as possible (recall matters to reduce readmissions); (3) AUPRC reflects the clinical decision-making trade-off without requiring pre-specified threshold assumptions.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- AUROC — discriminative ability across probability thresholds
- F1-score at validation-optimized threshold — numerical balance of precision and recall
- Sensitivity at 90% specificity — detection rate under controlled false positive constraints
- Brier score and calibration plots — alignment of predicted probabilities with observed readmission rates (critical for clinical deployment)

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for both models are tuned via grid search on the validation set (Sep–Dec 2022), using AUPRC as the selection criterion. The test set remains untouched during tuning. After finalization, both models are re-trained on the combined training + validation sets to maximize training signal, then evaluated once on the held-out test set.

### 7. Controlling for Confounds

**Socioeconomic Status and Follow-up Care Access:**
- Include postal code-level poverty index as a feature in both models
- Stratify test set results by insurance type (proxy for SES) and discharge disposition (proxy for follow-up access)
- Document post-discharge follow-up plans (PCP visits, home health referrals) and report separate performance metrics for patients with and without recorded plans
- Acknowledge unmeasured factors (informal support, patient compliance) and clearly scope the claim: the experiment demonstrates whether the RNN outperforms logistic regression *within the captured population*, but does not establish causal mechanisms or generalizability across health systems with different SES distributions or infrastructure.

### 8. Scope and Expectations

This design tests whether sequential symptom progression, when modeled by an RNN, improves 30-day readmission prediction over demographic and aggregate clinical features. Success is defined as higher AUPRC on the test set for the RNN. The experiment does not establish why the RNN may outperform (feature importance analysis is a separate inquiry), nor does it guarantee generalization to other health systems or patient populations with different readmission drivers.
